description: 45M fell off ladder, brief LOC, GCS 15 in ED — Canadian CT Head candidate
transcript:
Dr. Olsen: What happened?
Patient: I was on a ladder cleaning the gutters, maybe eight feet up. I lost my balance and fell. I hit the side of my head on the patio.
Dr. Olsen: Did you lose consciousness?
Wife: He was out for maybe a minute. I ran over and he was just lying there. He came around, but he kept asking me what happened, like three or four times.
Dr. Olsen: How long was he confused?
Wife: Maybe ten or fifteen minutes. He's fine now though.
Dr. Olsen: How are you feeling now, sir?
Patient: Headache, but I know what's going on. I know where I am.
Dr. Olsen: Any vomiting?
Patient: No.
Dr. Olsen: Any blood thinners?
Patient: No, nothing.
Dr. Olsen: How old are you?
Patient: Forty-five.
Dr. Olsen: Past medical history?
Patient: Healthy. No medications.
Nurse: GCS 15, alert and oriented x3. HR 78, BP 138/82, sat 99.
Dr. Olsen: Pupils equal and reactive. No focal deficits. Battle's sign negative, no raccoon eyes, no hemotympanum. Tender boggy area on the left temporal scalp, no obvious depression.
Dr. Olsen: He's got LOC and brief amnesia from a fall greater than three feet. I want to think about whether he meets criteria for imaging.

groundTruth.notes: Classic Canadian CT Head case. Minor head injury with witnessed LOC, GCS 15, no obvious high-risk findings on exam, plus a dangerous mechanism (fall from height). Doctor explicitly entering decision mode. Pre-fill should reflect what was heard; missing elements include anything not yet asked (vomiting count, age cutoff at 65).
